classicDemonstrations.title: Generate versus read
classicDemonstrations.summary: Learners are asked to complete a word, antonym, or phrase themselves in one condition and simply read the finished item in another. Later recall is usually stronger for the self-generated items.
classicDemonstrations.whyItMatters: The memory advantage comes from production and active retrieval work, not just from extra exposure time.

classicDemonstrations.title: Make your own example
classicDemonstrations.summary: Students who create their own example, label, or paraphrase of a concept tend to remember it better than peers who only receive a polished example from the teacher.
classicDemonstrations.whyItMatters: This turns the bias into a design principle for lessons, notes, and study prompts: asking for generation changes encoding.

classicDemonstrations.title: Does this describe me?
classicDemonstrations.summary: People judge whether a list of traits applies to themselves, to another person, or only to a surface feature like letter shape. Later recall is strongest for the self-related judgments.
classicDemonstrations.whyItMatters: The effect shows that self-reference is not just interesting content. It changes how deeply information is organized in memory.

classicDemonstrations.title: Personal connection encoding
classicDemonstrations.summary: A neutral fact or concept is remembered better when learners explicitly connect it to their own goals, history, or likely future use than when they rehearse it impersonally.
classicDemonstrations.whyItMatters: This is why reflection prompts that ask “What does this mean for me?” often outperform passive review.

summary: The tendency to remember your own relative standing in a way that preserves a familiar self-comparison story.

classicDemonstrations.title: Recalled comparative ratings
classicDemonstrations.summary: People rate themselves against peers on several traits or skills, then later reconstruct those ratings from memory. The remembered placements often come back cleaner and more self-consistent than the originals.
classicDemonstrations.whyItMatters: The bias is not only in the first comparison. Memory itself helps stabilize a flattering or identity-protective ranking.

classicDemonstrations.title: Easy versus hard ability lists
classicDemonstrations.summary: When the task list makes above-average or below-average judgments feel natural, people later remember their comparative placement in the same direction even when the original ratings were more mixed.
classicDemonstrations.whyItMatters: This shows how recall can preserve a social self-story rather than an exact record of earlier judgments.

summary: The tendency to distinguish faces of your own race more accurately than faces of other races.

appliedContexts.title: Lineup construction and administration
appliedContexts.summary: Cross-race identification calls for careful filler selection, blind administration, and clean witness instructions because the witness may have had weaker face-level discrimination from the start.
appliedContexts.whyItMatters: It shifts the focus from sincerity to conditions. A confident witness can still be working from a thinner perceptual record.

appliedContexts.title: Interview timing and exposure records
appliedContexts.summary: Investigators should record viewing conditions, delay, stress, and cross-race context before treating an identification as especially strong.
appliedContexts.whyItMatters: Reliability depends on exposure quality and delay, not just on whether the witness sounds certain afterward.

summary: The tendency for witnesses to remember more detail about someone of the same gender under some conditions.

appliedContexts.title: Interview design for witness recall
appliedContexts.summary: Interviewers should document what the witness actually had time and attention to encode instead of assuming the same level of person-detail recall across all targets.
appliedContexts.whyItMatters: The page is most useful as a reminder that recall quality can vary systematically with who was observed, not just with how honest the witness is.

appliedContexts.title: Lineup confidence versus detail quality
appliedContexts.summary: When person-memory detail varies by witness-target gender match, confidence should be checked against the original statement and exposure conditions rather than treated as a free-standing signal of accuracy.
appliedContexts.whyItMatters: This keeps the practical lesson on reliability, not on a simplistic claim that one witness group is universally better.
